coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dot-based sensors for food safety
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Tongxiang
Li, Zhao
Huang, Tianzi
Tian, Lin
coredata.dc:description: By taking advantages of the remarkable fluorescent properties, simple preparation strategy, good biocompatibility, and easy functionalization, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising materials for the application in the sensing, particularly for the food safety. Although great progress has been achieved in recent years, the low selectivity and sensitivity have severely hindered their extensive application as food samples are complex in compositions that may cause interferences. Therefore, more efforts are demanded to contribute to overcoming these issues. Previous researches have demonstrated that the sensing performance of CQDs is strongly correlated with the composites, in which the CQDs that combined with other materials to generate nanostructured composites with distinctive properties could exhibit excellent sensing performance. This article introduces and compares various CQD-based sensors and reviews their related sensing applications as a novel material in food components analysis and food safety inspection, including the fluorescence sensing, electrochemical sensing, and photochemical sensing. It is highly expected to afford a crucial guidance for the rational design of more advanced CQD-based sensors and boosting their applications in food safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Sensors
Fluorescence
Photoelectrochemical
Food safety

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Applications of diverse CQDs in the food detection

Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financed by The Xuzhou science and technology plan project of China ( KC20186 ), Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China ( 19KJB150019, 20KJB150043 ), Youth Science and Technology Talents Enrollment Project of the Jiangsu Association of Science and Technology .

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic description of the synthetic procedures of CQDs. (B) Fluorescent spectra of CQDs-MNPs in the presence of E. coli at different concentrations. (C) The linear plot between the fluorescent intensity and E. coli concentrations.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with the permission from [82], 2021, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The schemes for the preparation of N-doped CQDs and their applications for glutathione detection. (B) The excitation, fluorescence emission, and UV–vis adsorption spectra of the N-doped CQDs solution. (C) The fluorescence responses of N-doped CQDs in the presence of glutathione solution with different concentrations.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with the permission from [47], 2020, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustrating the synthetic procedures of the Ab-moderated WS2 nanosheets. (B) The scheme for illustrating the FRET-based duplex immunoassay for the chloramphenicol and amantadine residues detection.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with the permission from [6], 2020, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence quenching mechanism for N, S/CQDs for the detection of Hg2+.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with the permission from [85], 2015, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 5

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustrations of the mechanism for the improved photoresponsivity and (B) detailed response rate of ZnO/NCQDs under UV/visible light.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with the permission from [86], 2020, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 6

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustrating the synthetic procedures of the MIECS.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with the permission from [92], 2017, Elsevier. (B) Schematic representations of the preparation of CQDs and the grafting of CQDs on ITO and their application for the capsaicin determination. Reproduced with the permission from [93], 2020, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 7

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the electrochemical synthesis of the N-CQD/Cu2O composite for the sensitive detection of ASA in berries.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with the permission from [75], 2018, Elsevier.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 8

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustrating the synthetic process of Au/NGQDs composites. (B) The specific sensing process and (C) reaction mechanisms.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with the permission from [105], 2020, MDPI.

ce:figure.ce:label: Fig. 9

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication of PEC nanosensors and their application toward detecting zeatin based on g-C3N4 and DNA aptamer.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reproduced with the permission from [100], 2018, Elsevier.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the preparation of CQDs.
A comparison of different production methods of CQDs.
A comparison of the sensors based on CQDs for the application in the food safety.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dot-based sensors for food safety

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food (Biology) Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology, 221018, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food (Biology) Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology 221018, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology, 221018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Chemical Engineering, Xuzhou University of Technology 221018, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: By taking advantages of the remarkable fluorescent properties, simple preparation strategy, good biocompatibility, and easy functionalization, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as promising materials for the application in the sensing, particularly for the food safety. Although great progress has been achieved in recent years, the low selectivity and sensitivity have severely hindered their extensive application as food samples are complex in compositions that may cause interferences. Therefore, more efforts are demanded to contribute to overcoming these issues. Previous researches have demonstrated that the sensing performance of CQDs is strongly correlated with the composites, in which the CQDs that combined with other materials to generate nanostructured composites with distinctive properties could exhibit excellent sensing performance. This article introduces and compares various CQD-based sensors and reviews their related sensing applications as a novel material in food components analysis and food safety inspection, including the fluorescence sensing, electrochemical sensing, and photochemical sensing. It is highly expected to afford a crucial guidance for the rational design of more advanced CQD-based sensors and boosting their applications in food safety.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous improvement of people’s living standards, increasing attentions are focusing on their own health. Generally, food is essential to life and food safety is a basic human right, in which a safe food supply could help to greatly improve the human health, thereby increasing the length and quality of people’s lives [1–3]. As well known, the food chain starts from farm to fork/plate while challenges include microorganisms, hormones, etc., which may induce diverse food contamination that pose serious threats to human health, leading to millions of people become sick and die yearly [4–6]. Moreover, other contaminants including antibiotic and pesticide residues are challenges of food safety issues. Even more, the unsafety food may also provide a good way for the virus to spread, severely affecting the health and financial systems of world [7,8]. For example, the syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) could well spread out in the unsafety seafood, which induced unprecedented global pandemic of COVID-19 [7]. Therefore, food safety is no less significant today than ever and even become a predominately important global issue.

In the viewpoint of food safety, various efficient technologies have been well developed for the detection of food components and risk factors, including gas/liquid chromatography, enzyme-linked method, and immunological detection methods [9–12]. Owing to the high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of various analytes to ensure food safety, however, most of these techniques have been limited to the practical detection in the food samples because of their inherent disadvantages of complex sample treatment and time-consuming [13–16]. Moreover, these methods also require expensive instruments and well-trained persons [17–19]. Therefore, a huge gap is still existed between the practical detection of analytes and theoretical analyses.

With the rapid development of nanoscience, nanotechnologies have emerged as promising detection technology for the food safety, for which may provide an opportunity to conquer the certain challenges on food safety [20–22]. Compared to conventional detection methods, the nanomaterial-based sensors show the advantages of more specific target recognition, greatly enhanced selectivity and sensitivity, and increased signal readout [23–25]. As the central component of nanosensors, the nanomaterials play a crucial role in determining the sensing performance. In order to further improve the sensing performance [7,13,16–19,26–31], it is increasingly important to develop cost-effective nanomaterials. In regard to the nanomaterials for food safety, many effective nanomaterials have been developed, such as metallic nanoparticles [32], semiconductor [33], and carbon-based materials [34,35]. Among these nanomaterials, the carbon quantum dots (CQDs), also called carbon dots (CDs), have been considered as promising materials for food detection with ultrafine sizes below 10 nm [36–38]. It is because the CQDs are equipped with the merits of strong fluorescent intensity with low toxicity, good chemical stability and biocompatibility, excellent electron-transfer efficiency and photoinduced redox properties, and rich edge defects [39–41]. In addition, it is also demonstrated that the various functional groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy on the carbon core of CQDs make them soluble and appropriate for the further functionalization with different species [42–44]. Furthermore, doped CQDs with heteroatoms such as N, F, S, P, and B could also greatly moderate their sensing performance by optimizing their electronic structures [5,45–48]. These merits have enabled CQDs to be promising nanomaterials for the food detection.

Herein, in the review, we have reviewed the recent progress of CQDs in the field of food safety, with the emphasis on the synthesis of CQDs and the detection applications of diverse CQDs in food. With the information incorporated in this article, we will advise a future direction of the research in the fabrication of high-quality CQDs, mechanisms, and their applications in the efficient detection in food samples. Moreover, the perspectives associated with the future challenges of CQDs in this field are also manifested.

Generally speaking, the top-down approach refers to the physically or chemically “breaking” larger carbon structures (e.g., carbon nanotubes, activated carbon, graphite rods, carbon ash) into small-sized CQDs by arc discharge treatment [18,49,50]. With the rapid development of technology, the breaking method has been extended to laser ablation, acidic etching, and electrochemical oxidation at high potential [51–53]. Although the top-down approaches are effective for the synthesis of CQDs, these methods are commonly operated under the conditions of high acidity, high potential, and high energy, in which the reaction conditions are hard to control. As a result, the preparation of CQDs through these top-down methods is rarely reported.

In contrast to the top-down approach, the bottom-up method refers to the synthesis of CQDs by aggregating small organic or oligomer precursors. And the representative bottom-up approaches include the microwave, hydrothermal, and ultrasound methods [54,55]. Normally, under the environment of high radiation, high heat and frequency, the CQDs could be prepared and equipped with high quality and good optical properties. Compared to the top-down approach, these methods are simple and controllable, which could offer possibility for the further fabrication of a large amount of CQDs. In addition, the bottom-up approaches also include the chemical oxidation and hydrothermal/solvothermal methods. Owing to the unique physiochemical, photochemical, and biochemical properties, CQDs could be extensively applied in the field of biosensing, bioimaging, and photoelectrocatalysis.

Normally, according to the different carbon cores, CQDs can be classified into three types: carbon nanodots, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and polymer dots (PDs). As well known, carbon nanodots are quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles (<10 nm) without a distinct crystal lattice, which is dominantly comprised of sp2/sp3 carbons, as well as embedded sp2-hybridized nanocrystalline regions [15,56,57]. Different from carbon nanodots, GQDs are functionalized nanoframents of graphene with lateral size of generally below 10 nm. As a representative type of CQDs, GQDs also have the distinctive quantum effects, excellent photostability, as well as promising biocompatibility. In addition, the stacked graphene-like sp2 carbon-type layer structures within the GQDs also endowed them with distinctive quantum confinement and edge effects [58,59], thereby contributing a broad-band optical adsorption. As for PDs (<20−30 nm), they are prepared by aggregating or cross-linking the polymer nanoparticles that derived from the linear polymers or monomers [60].

Although a variety of CQDs have been developed, the optical properties of CQDs are similar in regard to the adsorption and photoluminsescence (PL), which have thus been widely investigated for improved applications. For the optical property, CQDs are commonly having a strong adsorption in the UV region of 260−320 nm. After a suitable surface passivation, the adsorption band could be broadened, which may provide functionalities for suitable application. For example, Wu et al. reported the fabrication of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) by easily tailoring the hydrothermal parameters with ethylenediamine (EDA) and citric acid (CA) as precursors [61]. Impressively, it is revealed that the strong synergistic effect between the carbogenic core and the surface/molecule state predominately contribute to the high photoluminescence quantum yield of 97.4 %. In addition to the PL, it is also revealed that CQDs also possess many other optical properties, including electrochemiluminescence (ECL), phosphorescence, and photo-induced electrode transfer (PER) properties. Generally, their unique optical properties could be well illustrated as follows. Owing to the electronic transitions from π to π* of the phenyl rings and CC bonds or generate n to π* of CO binds, the CQDs could thus display adsorption intensity of light.

In addition to the unique optical properties, CQDs have also been demonstrated to possess distinctive physicochemical properties. It is because CQDs can be synthesized by various approaches employing different chemicals. In spite of the different raw materials and different synthetic methods, CQDs are similar in terms of the physicochemical properties [38,62–64]. For one thing, CQDs have an excellent photostability with ultrastable fluorescence emission intensity after a continuous excitation treatment, resulting in the potential application in bioimaging [45,56,65,66]. For another, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs is strongly correlated with the pH of solution as the local surface environment could be greatly influenced by the ionization of the carboxylic-acid groups [67–70]. To this end, the PL can be switched off and on by easily tuning the pH values of the solution. Besides the favorable physicochemical properties, the low cytotoxicity is also a prerequisite for the practical applications in the food detections [71]. For the applications in biological systems, the biocompatibility is also of vital significance. Excepting for the unique optical and physicochemical properties, CQDs are also endowed with the excellent biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity.

As an important member of carbon family, CQDs are similar to other carbon-based materials in terms of the electrochemical properties. Besides the inherently electrochemical properties, CQDs also possess a series of other favorable merits in the application of electrochemical. First, CQDs possess a large specific surface area, which could provide abundant active centers for electrolyte and intermediates [72,73]. Second, the high electrical conductivity greatly boosts the efficient electron transfer during electrochemical reactions [37,74–76]. Third, the rich surface functional groups (OH, NH2, etc.) could enable to expose a large amount of active sites for enhancing electrocatalytic performance [39]. Fourth, the electronic structure of the CQDs could also be effectively tailored by the heteroatom doping, for which could greatly modify the electronic properties of carbon atoms through the intramolecular charge transfer [70]. All of these advantageous merits have enabled CQDs and their composites to be promising candidates for the future applications. Inspired by these beneficial terms, CQDs have thus been famously utilized in the field of electrocatalysis, energy storage, and electrochemical sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of diverse CQDs in the food detection

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are a novel class of excellent optical nanomaterials, with a large amount of advantages including low toxicity, low cost, and good biocompatibility [77–79]. These advantages have enabled CQDs to function as the fluorescence probes for the harmful substances and additives detection, such as foodborne pathogens, mycotoxins, and tartrazine [9,80]. In recent years, CQDs have been extensively utilized as the fluorescent materials for working as fluorescent probes in food analysis. Compared to other photoactive semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs are endowed with the advantages of good fluorescent properties, low toxicity, and good stability, boosting them as ideal materials for the applications of fluorescent detection [81]. For example, Hu et al. have reported a green one-step route for the fabrications of CQDs with an average diameter of 4.26 ± 0.3 nm through a facile microwave-assisted method (Fig. 1 A) [82]. It is revealed that the CQDs can exhibit the strong green fluorescence under the excitation wavelength of 420 nm, which could thus function as the fluorescent probe for the detection of E. coli. Consequently, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe was obviously decreased with the addition of E. coli (Fig. 1B), and linearity between E. coli concentration and fluorescent intensity was also constructed with an ultralow detection limit of 487 CFU/mL and a wide detection range of 500−106 CFU/mL (Fig. 1C). More importantly, the milk samples contaminated by E. coli could also be detected by this method, in which the performance was strongly agreed with that detected by plate-counting methods. By taking inspiration from the excellent PL properties of CQDs, Hu et al. have also realized the sensitive detection of trace substance in the food samples [47]. Specifically, they have utilized phenylenediamine as precursor and developed a facile solvothermal method for the synthesis of the unique N-doped CQDs, in which the highly soluble N-doped CQDs could display strong yellow emission under UV light. Remarkably, by mixing the N-doped CQDs with glutathione, it is interestingly to find that its fluorescence is dramatically quenched due to the static interaction. On the basis of this mechanism, a new fluorescence probe was thus fabricated and effective for the direct detection of glutathione (Fig. 2 A). Meanwhile, this fluorescence probe is also effective for the detection of glutathione in the fruit and vegetable samples with the good linear responses in concentration ranges of 0.1–1.0 μM and 1.0–220.0 μM, as well as the limits of detection of 0.059 μM and 5.54 μM, respectively (Fig. 2B, C).

Heteroatom (i.e., N, S, P, B) doping is another effective strategy for the sensing performance moderation of CQDs. Owing to the difference in electronegativity, the doped heteroatom could effectively induce the charge transfer from adjacent carbon atoms and structural distortion, which could thus regulate the electronic structure of CQDs and alter the local density of state, thereby optimizing the adsorption/desorption of intermediated species. These attractive properties have thus boosted the applications of heteroatom doped CQDs not only in the field of electrochemistry but also PL. For instance, Lv et al. have developed a simple pyrolytic synthetic method for the construction of highly crystalline N-doped CQDs employing tartaric acid and chitosan as the precursors [5]. Thanks to the chelation between Fe3+ and the surface functional groups of CQDs, the fluorescence of CQDs could be effectively quenched by Fe3+, while the other metal ions showed almost no influence on the fluorescence. Interestingly, a linear relationship between Fe3+ concentrations and the fluorescence of CQDs was established, a wide detection range of 0−145 μM and a low detection limitation of 1.15 μM is estimated. Moreover, by introducing (L-Ascorbic acid) AA, the fluorescence of CQDs quenched by Fe3+ could be apparently restored as the Fe3+ have been reduced by the introduced AA, which could thus be utilized for the detection of AA. Consequently, a detection range of 0−150 μM together with an ultralow detection limit of 0.02 μM is obtained. Furthermore, based on this detection mechanism, the AA in the fruit juices and vegetables could also be detected by such fluorescence method.

In addition to the modifications of the CQDs, coupling CQDs with other composites is also an effective approach for optimizing their sensing performance [83]. On the one hand, the inherent PL properties along with abundant advantages of CQDs enabled them to be a class of promising nanomaterials for the fluorescence detection. On the other hand, the synergistic effect between CQDs and other composite is also favorable for ultrasensitive detection of trace substances. For instance, Li et al. have constructed a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of Pb2+ in the porphyra based on the composite comprised of Cu nanoclusters and N-doped CQDs [46]. In this probe, the Cu nanoclusters could yield the response signal, and the fluorescence signal could be further enhanced by the detected Pb2+, while the N-doped CQDs offered a self-calibration signal. Based on the change of fluorescence intensity ratio in the fluorophores, this nanohybid could act as a ratiometric probe for realizing the ultrasensitive detection of Pb2+, with a detection limit of 0.0031 mg L−1.

Besides the metallic nanoclusters, coupling CQDs with transition metal dichalcogenides has also been considered as a promising approach for the achieving selective and sensitive detections of harmful substance in the food. For instance, Dong et al. have proposed a duplex and homogeneous immunoassay for realizing the simultaneous detection of chloramphenicol and amantadine residues detection in the chicken breast based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the antibody-moderated WS2 nanosheets and CQDs (Fig. 3 A) [6]. The blue and green-emissive CQDs were firstly synthesized through a facile one-pot method, leading to the formation of different degree of oxidation of CQDs. Interestingly, by coupling haptens to the CQDs, two independent energy donors are successfully fabricated, which could thus induce the efficient FRET-based quenching between the hapten-labeled CQDs and Ab-moderated WS2 nanosheets, thereby achieving rapid screening of drug residues in food (Fig. 3B) with a limit of detection of 0.06 ng g−1. These researches have extended the applications of CQDs in food analysis.

Generally, optical based analytical sensors provide a good choice of analysis as it is comprised of portable and simple instrumentation, rapid and strong response, and inexpensive reagents. In recent years, the optical based analytical sensors have increasingly attractive when combined with the nanotechnologies, especially for those nanoparticles with high specific surface area. Owing to the inherent advantages and good optical properties, CQDs have attracted increasingly more interests in the field of optical based analytical sensors. Generally, the luminescence sensors can be mainly classified into three types: photoluminescence chemosensors, chemiluminescence chemosensors, and electrochemiluminescence chemosensors [84]. All of these luminescence sensors have been demonstrated to be sensitive to the detection of targeted substance. Typically, the studies on the detection of harmful substances based on luminescence sensors are widely reported. Taking Zhang and Chen’s works as an example, they have found that the Hg2+ ions could effectively quench the fluorescence of N-doped CQDs due to the variation of surface states of N-doped CQDs that affected by Hg2+ [64]. The experimental data revealed that the sensor was selective for Hg2+, which could thus work as a probe for the selective detection of Hg2+. Similarly, a recent research has demonstrated that the N and S co-doped CQDs with high fluorescence quantum yields could also be utilized as the sensor probe for the highly sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+. As demonstrated by Li and coworkers, the co-doping of N and S atoms in the CQDs could greatly promote the electron-transfer rate and coordination interaction between Hg2+ and N, S/CQDs, leading to the high sensitivity and selectivity for the Hg2+ determination (Fig. 4 A, B) [85].

Combining the CQDs with other photoactive materials is also effective for further promoting the photodetection properties [54]. For one thing, both the CQDs and photoactive materials can function as the active centers for synergistically enhancing the photodetection properties. For another, the efficient charge transfer between CQDs and photoactive materials could further promote the photodetection performance. Guided by this rule, many advanced researches about the construction of CQDs-based nanocomposites have been reported. For example, Wang et al. have proposed a green route for the synthesis of NCQDs and incorporating them into ZnO nanorod arrays (Fig. 5 A) [86]. Thanks to the successful incorporation of NCQDs, the detectors have demonstrated broadband photoresponse with largely improved photoresponsivity, faster response, and much better sensitivity, leading them to be promising broadband photodetectors (Fig. 5B).

In recent years, electrochemical detection has also emerged as a promising strategy used in the food analysis. Compared to other methods, electrochemical detection methods are more powerful because of its plenty of advantages, including simple, fast analysis, and strong response [87–91]. Owing to these advantageous factors, electrochemical methods have been extensively researched and utilized for the sensitive detection of trace substance. In regard to the electrochemical sensors, the central component is the electrode material, in which the electrode material possess high electrochemical activity will be favorable for enhancing the electrochemical sensing performance. As a new carbon nanomaterial, CQDs have attracted increasing interests as electrode materials due to its high fluorescent, suitable conductivity, and low toxicity. Therefore, many effective electrochemical sensors that based on the CQDs have been successfully constructed. For example, Li et al. have fabricated the NCQDs decorated N-doped carbon nanofibers composite, which could be employed as efficient electrode material in the highly sensitive electrochemical sensors for the nitrite determination [92]. It is revealed that the NCQDs grafted on the surface N-doped carbon nanofibers could effectively avoid the serious aggregation and thus maintain the high specific surface area. Moreover, such N-doped carbon nanofibers could also work as the ideal support for NCQDs attachment to benefit for the charge transfer. Thanks to these advantages, it is demonstrated that the electrochemical sensors based on this composite display superior analysis performance, with a wide linear range, ultralow detection limit, as well good reproducibility and selectivity.

More recently, Zheng et al. have fabricated a novel molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor (MIECS) based on GCE that modified with the CQDs and chitosan (CS) for the highly sensitive determination of glucose (Fig. 6 A) [92]. In the MIECS, the application of CQDs and CS greatly increase the surface active area and substantially improve the electron-transport capability. As a result, the proposed MIECS demonstrated high selectivity, high sensitivity, excellent electrochemical stability, and reproducibility. The detection limit was measured to be as low as 0.09 μM for glucose determination by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). Furthermore, the proposed MIECS could be employed to a preliminary test for glucose analysis in human blood, which showed great promise for the further application in the hospital’s instruments. CQDs have also been employed as the electrode materials in the electrochemical probes for the determination of capsaicin. As developed by Supchocksoonthorn and coworkers, they have synthesized the CQDs from iota-carrageenan employing a simple hydrothermal approach (Fig. 6B) [93]. For fabricating the electrodes, the CQDs are grafted onto an indium tin oxide (ITO) through a one-step thermal annealing strategy. In this system, the CQDs significantly facilitate the electron transfer between capsaicin and electrode. Moreover, the synergistically favorable energy levels and improved interactions also contributed to the enhanced selective capsaicin sensing. As a consequence, a wide linear range of 0.05−500 μM and an ultralow detection limit of 5.4 nM are achieved for the detection of capsaicin based on carbon dot-grafted ITO electrode.

In addition to the detection of harmful substances in the food samples, electrochemical sensors are also demonstrated to be highly efficient for the detection of drugs in the berries. For instance, Muthusankar et al. have proposed a simple electrochemical preparation strategy for the precise deposition of N-CQDs on the low index Cu crystal to greatly match and promote the stability of Cu2O (Fig. 7 ) [75]. By modifying the GCE with N-CQD and Cu2O, they have prepared the working electrode to greatly boost the electrochemical oxidation of Aspirin (ASA) with higher current response as compared to the N-CQD/GCE, Cu2O/GCE, and bare GCE. Furthermore, a good linear range of 1−907 μM with a low detection limit of around 1−907 μM, as well as the sensitivity of around 21.87 μA μM−1 cm−2.

Recently, photo-electrochemical (PEC) detection has emerged as potential approach for realizing the accurate and selective detection of trace harmful substances in the food [77,94–96]. Compared to the tradition detection methods and electrochemical detection, PEC possesses more advantages, including a much wider detection range, higher sensitivity, and lower detection limit [22,76,97]. In spite of the great advantages, the limitations of photoactive materials severely impeded their extensive applications. Inspired by the great success achieved in the field of PEC, numerous efforts have been devoted to the rational design and fabrication of highly photoactive materials [95,98–100]. Therefore, many photoactive materials have been successfully developed, including TiO2, C3N4, MoS2, and transition metal composites [101–104]. Recently, CQDs, which are endowed with distinctive quantum effects, good biocompatibility, outstanding photostability, have thus been extensively utilized in the fields of photocatalysis, electrocatalysis, and biosenseing. By taking full advantages of the CQDs to construct a PEC sensor is highly imperative for realizing the efficient detection of targeted substance. For example, Zhao et al. have coupled N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) with Au to modify electrode and employed them as “on-off” nanosensors for the PEC detection of caffeic acid under visible-light irradiation (Fig. 8 A) [105]. It was revealed that the NCQDs could adsorb light energy to generate NGQDs* free radicals for caffeic acid oxidation. Meanwhile, the Au nanoparticles could be excited and form a local surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) effect, leading to the quick distinguish of caffeic acid (Fig. 8B, C). Moreover, the synergistic effect between Au nanoparticles and NGQDs could also greatly accelerate the PEC process, resulting in highly sensitive performance. Consequently, as determined by i-t test, the limit of detection was measured to be as low as 0.03 μM, together with a wide linear range of 0.11−30.25 μM.

As an important member of the carbon family, CQDs have the inherent characteristics of carbon-based materials, including high electrical conductivity, large specific surface area, and structural robustness. In addition, it has been demonstrated that the CQDs also possess the unique features of outstanding fluorescent, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, as well as cheap preparation. In combination with the inherent properties of the carbon-based nanomaterials with the unique optical and biological properties, CQDs have thus attracted increasingly interests and became a hotspot research, especially in the field of biosensor. In this review article, we have summarized the current advances of the CQDs relating to their synthesis, functionalization, and application to the food analysis. After review a large amount of literatures, we found that the many exciting research results, which demonstrated that CDs could provide an opportunity for the effective detection of targeted substances in the food samples. In spite of the great advantages of CQDs for food analysis, the progress in CQDs research is still in its early stage, and many important challenges are highly imperative to be addressed.

First, although many effective strategies have been developed for the preparation of CQDs, the lack of effective approaches for the scalable production of CQDs with desirable structures (i.e., size, shape, and crystallinity) has significantly limited their extensive applications. Previous researches have referred to the purity of CQDs samples, where many byproducts such as carbon particles and oligomers would generate. The bottom-up method used to yield impurities from the reaction precursors. These issues, together with the low degree of the control of CQDs severely impeded their practical applications in the food analysis and other important fields.

Second, despite the CQDs are revealed to possess high fluorescent quantum yields, they are much lower than that of semiconductor quantum dots. In order to realize the practical fluorescent detection of food samples based on CQDs, an in-depth mechanism study is highly imperative. And more effective strategies for enhancing the fluorescent quantum yields are necessary to developed.

Third, in consideration of the high complexity of the food samples, a much stronger response should be obtained for the effective detection of trace substance in the food samples, which thus require CQDs-based sensors to possess not only high specificity but also high sensitivity. Therefore, more efforts should be devoted to optimizing the CQDs-based sensors to improve their selectivity and sensitivity for the simultaneous detection of multiple targets in one food sample.

Fourth, CQDs display distinguishable superiority in photo-electrochemical reaction as they combine the electrical properties of carbon materials with the unique optical properties, which also enabled CQDs to be promising electrode materials for PEC sensors. However, the PEC response of CQDs is not high enough for the practical detection in the food samples. In order to address these issues and improve the PEC signals, coupling or hybridizing CQDs with photoactive species is an effective approach, where the photoactive species could offer fast electron tunnels to facilitate the charge separation and transport, thereby enhancing the PEC performance.

Though far from the full exploitation, the plenty of remarkable features enable the CQDs to be promising nanomaterials in the field of food analysis. With the rapid development of nanoscience and nanotechnology, we envision that more high-quality CQDs and CQDs-based composites will be developed for significantly boosting their practical application in the food analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors report no declarations of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financed by The Xuzhou science and technology plan project of China (KC20186), Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (19KJB150019<GN2>, <GN2>20KJB150043), Youth Science and Technology Talents Enrollment Project of the Jiangsu Association of Science and Technology.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tongxiang Li received his Ph.D. from Southeast University in 2004. He carried out postdoctoral research in the Lanzhou University. He is a professor of Xuzhou University of Technology. His study interests mainly focus on the application of carbon quantum dots in food detection such as heavy metals, food additives, etc.

Zhao Li received his Ph.D. degree from the China University of Mining and Technology in 2019. Currently, she is a lecturer in the School of Materials and Chemical Engineering at Xuzhou University of Technology. Her reaearch is focused on the fabrication of Photo-Eletrochemical nanomaterials.

Tianzi Huang received his B.S. degree from the Jiangsu Normal University in 2012. Currently, she is a lecturer in the School of Food (Biology) Engineering at Xuzhou University of Technology. Her reaearch is focused on the Luminescence detection of food additives based carbon dots.

Lin Tian received his Ph.D. degree from the China University of Mining and Technology in 2017. Currently, he is a lecturer in the School of Materials and Chemical Engineering at Xuzhou University of Technology. He mainly focuses on the development of carbon-based nanocompsites for electrochemical applications, such as electrocatalysis, supercapacitor, and electrochemical sensing.
